news_summary: Breakthroughs in cancer treatment, early disease detection, organ transplantation, and allergy prevention highlight significant advancements in health today.
news_summary_fr: Les avancées dans le traitement du cancer, le dépistage précoce des maladies, la transplantation d'organes et la prévention des allergies soulignent les progrès significatifs réalisés aujourd'hui dans le domaine de la santé.
news_summary_es: Los avances en el tratamiento del cáncer, la detección precoz de enfermedades, el trasplante de órganos y la prevención de alergias ponen de relieve los importantes progresos que se han logrado en el ámbito de la salud en la actualidad.

articles.title: Scientists discover the switch that revives exhausted cancer-fighting T cells
articles.summary: Scientists have uncovered new genetic rules that determine whether the immune system’s “killer” T cells remain powerful long-term defenders or become worn out and ineffective. By building a detailed genetic atlas of CD8 T cell states, researchers identified key molecular switches that push these cells toward either resilience or exhaustion. Remarkably, disabling just two previously unknown genes restored the tumor-killing power of exhausted T cells while preserving their ability to provide lasting immune protection.
articles.content:
Researchers from the Salk Institute for Biological Studies, UNC Lineberger Comprehensive Cancer Center, and UC San Diego have identified new genetic mechanisms that influence how key immune cells decide their fate. These cells, known as CD8 "killer" T cells, can either develop into durable defenders that provide lasting protection or fall into a weakened state known as exhaustion. The study found that switching off just two genes can restore the ability of exhausted T cells to attack tumors.

The research, published in Nature, provides a framework that may allow scientists to deliberately program T cells so they maintain both long-term immune memory and strong cancer-fighting activity. The findings could have significant implications for cancer immunotherapy as well as treatments for infectious diseases.

CD8 killer T cells are vital to the immune system because they locate and destroy virus infected cells and cancer cells. However, when the immune system faces long lasting infections or tumors, these cells can gradually lose their effectiveness. Over time they can enter a dysfunctional condition called T cell exhaustion, where their ability to eliminate threats declines.

Building a Genetic Atlas of T Cell States

Protective T cells and exhausted ones can appear nearly identical, which makes them difficult to distinguish using traditional methods. To address this challenge, researchers explored whether these different states could be separated based on genetic activity.

A major breakthrough came from constructing a detailed genetic atlas that maps a range of CD8 T cell states. This atlas shows how these immune cells shift along a spectrum that runs from highly protective to severely impaired.

"Our long-term goal is to make immune therapies work better by creating clear 'recipes' for designing T cells," says co-corresponding author Susan Kaech, PhD, a professor at the Salk Institute at the time of the study. "To do that, we first needed to identify which molecular ingredients are uniquely active in one T cell state but not others. By building a comprehensive atlas of CD8 T cell states, we were able to pinpoint the key factors that define protective versus dysfunctional programs -- information that is essential for precisely engineering effective immune responses."

Can T Cell Exhaustion Be Reversed?

To understand how these immune states are controlled, the researchers examined nine distinct CD8 T cell conditions using advanced laboratory methods, genetic tools, mouse models, and computational analysis. Their work revealed several transcription factors, proteins that regulate gene activity, which act as switches that guide T cells toward either sustained function or exhaustion.

Among these regulators, the scientists identified two transcription factors called ZSCAN20 and JDP2 that had not previously been associated with T cell exhaustion. When these genes were disabled, exhausted T cells recovered their tumor killing ability while still maintaining long-term immune memory.

"We flipped specific genetic switches in the T cells to see if we could restore their tumor-killing function without damaging their ability to provide long-term immune protection," says co-corresponding author H. Kay Chung, PhD, an assistant professor at UNC Lineberger. Chung began this research at the Salk Institute before joining UNC. "We found that it was indeed possible to separate these two outcomes."

These findings challenge a long-standing assumption that immune exhaustion is an unavoidable result of prolonged immune activity.

Engineering Stronger Immune Cells for Cancer Therapy

The researchers say the genetic atlas they created could help guide the design of more powerful immune cells for treatments such as adoptive cell transfer (ACT) and CAR T cell therapy.

"Once we had this map, we could start giving T cells much clearer instructions -- helping them keep the traits that allow them to fight cancer or infection over the long term, while avoiding the pathways that cause them to burn out," says Kaech. "By separating these two programs, we can begin to design immune cells that are both durable and effective in cancer and chronic infection."

The discovery could be especially important for treating solid tumors, where immune exhaustion often limits the success of therapy.

AI and Future Strategies for Precision Immune Engineering

In future work, the team plans to combine advanced experimental techniques with AI guided computational modeling. Their goal is to develop many more precise genetic "recipes" that can program T cells into specific functional states, improving the precision of cellular therapies.

"Because genes work together in complex regulatory networks that are difficult to decipher, powerful computational tools are essential to pinpoint which regulators drive specific cell states," says co-corresponding author Wei Wang, PhD, a professor at UC San Diego. "This study shows that we can begin to precisely manipulate immune cell fates and unlock new possibilities for enhancing immune therapies."

By uncovering how killer T cells choose between resilience and exhaustion, the research moves scientists closer to deliberately guiding immune responses instead of watching them weaken during prolonged disease.

Other authors include Eduardo Casillas, Ming Sun, Shixin Ma, Shirong Tan, Brent Chick, Victoria Tripple, Bryan McDonald, Qiyuan Yang, Timothy Chen, Siva Karthik Varanasi, Michael LaPorte, Thomas H. Mann, Dan Chen, Filipe Hoffmann, Josephine Ho, April Williams, and Diana C. Hargreaves of Salk; Cong Liu, Alexander N. Jambor, Z. Audrey Wang, Jun Wang, Zhen Wang, Jieyuan Liu, and Zhiting Hu of UC San Diego; Anamika Battu, Brandon M. Pratt, Fucong Xie, Brian P. Riesenberg, Elisa Landoni, Yanpei Li, Qidang Ye, Daniel Joo, Jarred Green, Zaid Syed, Nolan J. Brown, Matthew Smith, Jennifer Modliszewski, Yusha Liu, Ukrae H. Cho, Gianpietro Dotti, Barbara Savoldo, Jessica E. Thaxton, and J. Justin Milner of UNC; Peixiang He, Longwei Liu, and Yingxiao Wang of University of Southern California; and Yiming Gao of Texas A&M University.

The work was supported by the National Institutes of Health (R37AI066232, R01AI123864, R21AI151986, R01CA240909, R01AI150282, R01HG009626, K01EB034321, R01AI177864, R01CA248359, R01CA244361, AI151123, EB029122, GM140929) and the Damon Runyon Cancer Research Foundation.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough where researchers identified genetic switches that can revive exhausted cancer-fighting T cells, restoring their tumor-killing ability while preserving long-term immune protection. This discovery has broad implications for improving cancer immunotherapy and treatments for infectious diseases, offering hope for more effective therapies against solid tumors and chronic infections. The detailed genetic atlas and potential for precise immune cell engineering represent a substantial advancement with meaningful real-world impact on public health.
articles.personality_title: Scientists find genetic switches that revive tired cancer-fighting T cells
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Our immune system has special cells called CD8 killer T cells that fight infections and cancer. But when these cells work too long, they get tired and stop working well. This tiredness is called T cell exhaustion, and it makes fighting diseases harder.

**What happened** – Scientists from several research centers studied these T cells and made a detailed genetic map showing how they change from strong to worn out. They discovered two genes, named ZSCAN20 and JDP2, that act like switches controlling this change. When researchers turned off these two genes in exhausted T cells, the cells regained their ability to kill cancer cells while still remembering how to protect the body long-term.

**Impact** – This discovery is important because it shows that T cell exhaustion can be reversed, which was not known before. It means scientists can now work on making immune cells stronger and longer-lasting. This could improve treatments for cancer, especially hard-to-treat solid tumors, and also help fight long-lasting infections.

**What's next step** – The research team plans to use artificial intelligence (AI) and advanced experiments to create precise genetic instructions for T cells. This will help design better immune therapies that keep T cells powerful without wearing out. These new methods could lead to more effective and lasting treatments for cancer and other diseases.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists discovered how to switch on tired cancer-fighting T cells again by turning off two genes, opening the door to stronger, longer-lasting immune therapies.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques découvrent des interrupteurs génétiques qui réveillent les cellules T épuisées combattant le cancer
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Notre système immunitaire possède des cellules spéciales appelées cellules T CD8 tueuses qui combattent les infections et le cancer. Mais quand ces cellules travaillent trop longtemps, elles s’épuisent et fonctionnent moins bien. Cette fatigue s’appelle l’épuisement des cellules T, ce qui rend la lutte contre les maladies plus difficile.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des scientifiques de plusieurs centres de recherche ont étudié ces cellules T et créé une carte génétique détaillée montrant comment elles passent de fortes à épuisées. Ils ont découvert deux gènes, nommés ZSCAN20 et JDP2, qui agissent comme des interrupteurs contrôlant ce changement. Quand ils ont désactivé ces deux gènes dans des cellules T épuisées, les cellules ont retrouvé leur capacité à tuer les cellules cancéreuses tout en gardant la mémoire de protection à long terme.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est importante car elle montre que l’épuisement des cellules T peut être inversé, ce qui n’était pas connu auparavant. Cela signifie que les scientifiques peuvent maintenant travailler à rendre les cellules immunitaires plus fortes et durables. Cela pourrait améliorer les traitements du cancer, notamment les tumeurs solides difficiles à soigner, et aider à combattre les infections persistantes.

**Prochaine étape** – L’équipe de recherche prévoit d’utiliser l’intelligence artificielle (IA) et des expériences avancées pour créer des instructions génétiques précises pour les cellules T. Cela aidera à concevoir de meilleures thérapies immunitaires qui maintiennent les cellules T puissantes sans les épuiser. Ces nouvelles méthodes pourraient aboutir à des traitements plus efficaces et durables contre le cancer et d’autres maladies.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Les scientifiques ont découvert comment réactiver les cellules T épuisées combattant le cancer en désactivant deux gènes, ouvrant la voie à des thérapies immunitaires plus fortes et durables.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos descubren interruptores genéticos que reviven células T agotadas que combaten el cáncer
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Nuestro sistema inmunológico tiene células especiales llamadas células T CD8 asesinas que luchan contra infecciones y cáncer. Pero cuando estas células trabajan mucho tiempo, se cansan y dejan de funcionar bien. Esta fatiga se llama agotamiento de las células T, y dificulta combatir enfermedades.

**Qué pasó** – Científicos de varios centros de investigación estudiaron estas células T y crearon un mapa genético detallado que muestra cómo cambian de fuertes a agotadas. Descubrieron dos genes, llamados ZSCAN20 y JDP2, que actúan como interruptores que controlan este cambio. Cuando apagaron estos dos genes en células T agotadas, las células recuperaron su capacidad para matar células cancerosas y conservar su memoria para proteger el cuerpo a largo plazo.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento es importante porque muestra que el agotamiento de las células T puede revertirse, algo que no se sabía antes. Esto significa que los científicos pueden trabajar para hacer las células inmunes más fuertes y duraderas. Podría mejorar los tratamientos contra el cáncer, especialmente en tumores sólidos difíciles de tratar, y también ayudar a combatir infecciones prolongadas.

**Próximo paso** – El equipo planea usar inteligencia artificial (IA) y experimentos avanzados para crear instrucciones genéticas precisas para las células T. Esto ayudará a diseñar mejores terapias inmunológicas que mantengan las células T potentes sin agotarlas. Estos nuevos métodos podrían llevar a tratamientos más efectivos y duraderos para el cáncer y otras enfermedades.

**Resumen en una frase** – Científicos descubrieron cómo reactivar células T agotadas que combaten el cáncer apagando dos genes, abriendo la puerta a terapias inmunes más fuertes y duraderas.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a vibrant, glowing network of intertwined DNA strands and gene switches shaped like delicate keys unlocking radiant, shield-like T cells that transform from dim, exhausted forms into bright, dynamic defenders, set against a soft, natural background of flowing cellular patterns symbolizing immune resilience and renewal.

articles.title: AI blood test finds silent liver disease years before symptoms
articles.summary: Researchers created an AI-driven liquid biopsy that scans patterns in fragments of DNA circulating in the blood. The system detected early liver fibrosis and cirrhosis—conditions that often go unnoticed until serious damage occurs. By analyzing genome-wide DNA fragmentation patterns rather than specific mutations, the approach captures hidden signals about a person’s overall health. Early detection could help doctors treat liver disease sooner and potentially prevent cancer.
articles.content:
Researchers at the Johns Hopkins Kimmel Cancer Center have developed an artificial intelligence (AI) driven liquid biopsy that analyzes genome wide patterns of cell free DNA (cfDNA) fragments circulating in the blood. The test examines how these DNA pieces break apart and where they appear across the genome. Using this information, the system can identify early signs of liver fibrosis and cirrhosis and may also detect broader indicators of chronic disease.

The study, partly funded by the National Institutes of Health, was published March 4 in Science Translational Medicine. It marks the first time that this type of DNA fragmentation analysis, known as fragmentome technology, has been systematically applied to detecting chronic diseases unrelated to cancer. Previously, the approach had mainly been investigated as a method for finding cancer.

Genome Wide DNA Fragment Patterns Reveal Disease Signals

Liquid biopsies that measure cfDNA have already shown promise for identifying cancer. However, scientists have not widely explored their potential for diagnosing other illnesses. In this new research, investigators performed whole genome sequencing on cfDNA samples from 1,576 individuals with liver disease and additional medical conditions. By examining DNA fragments across the entire genome, they searched for patterns that might signal disease.

The team analyzed both the size of DNA fragments and their distribution throughout the genome, including repetitive DNA regions that have rarely been studied. Each analysis included about 40 million fragments spanning thousands of genomic regions, producing an enormous dataset compared with most liquid biopsy tests.

Machine learning algorithms processed this information to identify fragmentation patterns linked to disease. Using these patterns, researchers created a classification system that detected early liver disease, advanced fibrosis and cirrhosis with high sensitivity.

"This builds directly on our earlier fragmentome work in cancer, but now using AI and genome-wide fragmentation profiles of cell-free DNA to focus on chronic diseases," says Victor Velculescu, M.D., Ph.D., co-director of the cancer genetics and epigenetics program at the Johns Hopkins Kimmel Cancer Center and co-senior author of the study. "For many of these illnesses, early detection could make a profound difference, and liver fibrosis and cirrhosis are important examples. Liver fibrosis is reversible in early its stages, but if left undetected, it can progress to cirrhosis and ultimately increase the risk of liver cancer."

Why DNA Fragment Analysis Is Different

Unlike many liquid biopsy methods that search for specific cancer related gene mutations, the fragmentome approach focuses on how DNA fragments are cut, packaged and distributed throughout the genome. According to the researchers, this broader view makes the method applicable to conditions beyond cancer, including diseases that can eventually raise cancer risk. The study was also co led by Robert Scharpf, Ph.D., professor of oncology, and Jill Phallen, Ph.D., assistant professor of oncology.

"The fact that we are not looking for individual mutations is what makes this study so powerful," says first author Akshaya Annapragada, an M.D./Ph.D. student in the Velculescu lab. "We are analyzing the entire fragmentome, which contains a tremendous amount of information about a person's physiologic state. The scale of these data, coupled with machine learning, enables development of specific classifiers for many different health conditions."

Early Detection Could Benefit Millions at Risk

Velculescu notes that roughly 100 million people in the United States have liver conditions that increase their risk of cirrhosis and liver cancer. Current blood based tests for fibrosis often lack sensitivity, especially in early stages of disease. Standard blood markers typically fail to detect early fibrosis and identify cirrhosis only about half the time. Imaging techniques such as specialized ultrasound or magnetic resonance scans can help, but these tools require equipment that is not always available.

"Many individuals at risk don't know they have liver disease," Velculescu says. "If we can intervene earlier -- before fibrosis progresses to cirrhosis or cancer -- the impact could be substantial."

He adds that identifying these precursor conditions early may allow doctors to treat underlying diseases sooner and potentially prevent cancer from developing.

Study Origins and the Fragmentome Comorbidity Index

The research grew out of a 2023 Cancer Discovery study led by Velculescu that focused on the fragmentome of liver cancer. While studying patients with liver tumors, the scientists noticed that some individuals with fibrosis or cirrhosis showed mostly normal fragmentation profiles but contained subtle DNA signals linked to disease. This observation prompted the team to examine the fragmentome patterns associated specifically with liver fibrosis and cirrhosis.

In another analysis involving 570 people with suspected serious illness, researchers created a fragmentation comorbidity index. This measure distinguished individuals with high and low Charlson Comorbidity Index scores, a widely used metric that estimates how additional health conditions affect a person's risk of death. The fragmentome based index predicted overall survival independently and in some cases proved more specific than traditional inflammatory markers. Certain fragmentation signatures also appeared to be associated with poorer clinical outcomes.

"The fragmentome can serve as a foundation for building different classifiers for different diseases, and importantly, these classifiers are disease-specific and do not cross-react," Annapragada says. "A liver fibrosis classifier is distinct from a cancer classifier. This is a unique, disease-specific test built from the same underlying platform."

Potential to Detect Other Chronic Diseases

The study also included people at elevated risk for a range of medical conditions. Researchers observed fragmentome signals linked to cardiovascular, inflammatory and neurodegenerative disorders. However, the study population did not include enough cases to build separate disease classifiers for each of these conditions. Instead, the findings suggest that the technology may eventually have wider medical applications, which researchers plan to investigate in future work.

The liver fibrosis assay described in the study remains a prototype and has not yet been introduced as a clinical test. The team's next steps involve refining and validating the classifier for liver disease and exploring fragmentome signatures connected to other chronic illnesses.

Researchers and Funding

Along with Velculescu, Annapragada, Scharpf and Phallen, the research team included Zachariah Foda, Hope Orjuela, Carter Norton, Shashikant Koul, Noushin Niknafs, Sarah Short, Keerti Boyapati, Adrianna Bartolomucci, Dimitrios Mathios, Michael Noe, Chris Cherry, Jacob Carey, Alessandro Leal, Bryan Chesnick, Nic Dracopoli, Jamie Medina, Nicholas Vulpescu, Daniel Bruhm, Sarah Bacus, Vilmos Adleff, Amy Kim, Stephen Baylin, Gregory Kirk, Andrei Sorop, Razvan Iacob, Speranta Iacob, Liana Gheorghe, Simona Dima, Katherine McGlynn, Manuel Ramirez-Zea, Claus Feltoft, Julia Johansen and John Groopman.

Funding for the research came in part from the Dr. Miriam and Sheldon G. Adelson Medical Research Foundation, SU2C in-Time Lung Cancer Interception Dream Team Grant, Stand Up to Cancer-Dutch Cancer Society International Translational Cancer Research Dream Team Grant, the Gray Foundation, The Honorable Tina Brozman Foundation, the Commonwealth Foundation, the Mark Foundation for Cancer Research, the Danaher Foundation and ARCS Metro Washington Chapter, the Family of Dan Y. Zhang AACR Scholar in Training Award, the Cole Foundation and National Institutes of Health grants CA121113, CA006973, CA233259, CA062924, CA271896, T32GM136577, T32GM148383 and DA036297.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant medical breakthrough where AI-driven liquid biopsy technology can detect early liver disease years before symptoms appear. This early detection has the potential to improve treatment outcomes and prevent progression to severe conditions like cirrhosis and liver cancer, benefiting millions at risk. The study is well-detailed, funded by reputable institutions, and highlights a novel application of genome-wide DNA fragmentation analysis beyond cancer detection, indicating broad and meaningful health impact.
articles.personality_title: AI blood test detects early liver disease years before symptoms
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Liver diseases like fibrosis and cirrhosis often go unnoticed until they cause serious damage. Current tests can miss early signs, making it hard for doctors to treat patients before the disease gets worse.

**What happened** – Researchers at Johns Hopkins developed a new blood test using artificial intelligence (AI) that looks at patterns in tiny DNA fragments floating in the blood. Instead of searching for specific gene changes, this test studies how DNA breaks apart across the whole genome. By analyzing these patterns in over 1,500 people, the AI system could detect early liver disease and cirrhosis with high accuracy.

**Impact** – This method is different because it captures hidden signals about a person’s overall health, not just cancer-related changes. Detecting liver disease early means doctors could start treatment sooner, possibly reversing damage and preventing liver cancer. Since many people with liver problems don’t know they have them, this test could help millions avoid severe illness.

**What’s next step** – The test is still a prototype. Scientists plan to improve and confirm its accuracy for liver disease and explore if the same approach can detect other chronic illnesses like heart or brain diseases. This could lead to new ways to find and treat many health problems early.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A new AI blood test can find hidden signs of liver disease years before symptoms appear, offering a chance for earlier treatment and better health outcomes.
articles.personality_title_fr: Un test sanguin à base d'IA détecte les maladies du foie avant les symptômes
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les maladies du foie comme la fibrose et la cirrhose passent souvent inaperçues jusqu’à causer de graves dégâts. Les tests actuels manquent souvent les premiers signes, compliquant le traitement précoce.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de Johns Hopkins ont créé un nouveau test sanguin utilisant l’intelligence artificielle (IA) qui analyse les motifs des petits fragments d’ADN circulant dans le sang. Plutôt que de chercher des mutations spécifiques, ce test étudie comment l’ADN se casse sur tout le génome. En analysant ces motifs chez plus de 1 500 personnes, l’IA a pu détecter la maladie du foie et la cirrhose à un stade précoce avec une grande précision.

**Impact** – Cette méthode est différente car elle révèle des signaux cachés sur la santé globale d’une personne, pas seulement sur le cancer. Détecter tôt la maladie du foie permettrait aux médecins de commencer un traitement plus rapidement, pouvant inverser les dégâts et éviter le cancer du foie. Beaucoup de personnes ignorent leur maladie du foie, ce test pourrait donc aider des millions à éviter des complications graves.

**Prochaine étape** – Le test est encore au stade prototype. Les scientifiques veulent améliorer sa précision pour le foie et voir s’il peut aussi détecter d’autres maladies chroniques comme celles du cœur ou du cerveau. Cela pourrait ouvrir la voie à de nouvelles méthodes pour diagnostiquer et traiter tôt plusieurs maladies.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Un nouveau test sanguin à base d’IA peut détecter des signes cachés de maladie du foie bien avant l’apparition des symptômes, offrant une chance de traitement plus précoce et de meilleurs résultats de santé.
articles.personality_title_es: Prueba de sangre con IA detecta enfermedad hepática temprana antes de síntomas
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Las enfermedades del hígado como la fibrosis y la cirrosis a menudo no se detectan hasta que causan daños graves. Las pruebas actuales suelen no identificar los primeros signos, dificultando el tratamiento oportuno.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de Johns Hopkins desarrollaron una nueva prueba de sangre que usa inteligencia artificial (IA) para analizar patrones en pequeños fragmentos de ADN que circulan en la sangre. En lugar de buscar cambios específicos en los genes, esta prueba estudia cómo se fragmenta el ADN en todo el genoma. Analizando estos patrones en más de 1,500 personas, el sistema de IA pudo detectar enfermedad hepática temprana y cirrosis con alta precisión.

**Impacto** – Este método es diferente porque capta señales ocultas sobre la salud general, no solo cambios relacionados con el cáncer. Detectar la enfermedad hepática temprano permite a los médicos comenzar el tratamiento antes, posiblemente revirtiendo el daño y previniendo el cáncer de hígado. Muchas personas no saben que tienen problemas hepáticos, por lo que esta prueba podría ayudar a millones a evitar enfermedades graves.

**Próximo paso** – La prueba aún es un prototipo. Los científicos planean mejorar y validar su precisión para la enfermedad hepática y explorar si el mismo método puede detectar otras enfermedades crónicas como las cardíacas o cerebrales. Esto podría abrir nuevas formas de diagnosticar y tratar problemas de salud temprano.

**Resumen en una frase** – Una nueva prueba de sangre con IA puede encontrar señales ocultas de enfermedad hepática años antes de que aparezcan síntomas, ofreciendo la oportunidad de un tratamiento más temprano y mejores resultados de salud.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a glowing, stylized human liver composed of interwoven, translucent DNA strands gently floating within a softly illuminated bloodstream, with delicate, abstract patterns of fragmented DNA pieces and subtle circuit-like lines symbolizing AI analysis surrounding it, all rendered in natural, calming earth tones and soft blues.

articles.title: Genetically modified pig liver keeps man alive until human organ transplant
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 06 March 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00736-0First time the procedure has been performed on a living person.
articles.content:
A medical team at Xijing Hospital of the Air Force Medical University in Xi’an, China, surgically connected a man with liver failure to an external, genetically modified pig liver. Credit: Xinhua via Alamy

A 56-year-old man with liver failure has become the first living person to be surgically connected to a genetically modified pig liver, say the team that conducted the surgery. The pig organ filtered the man’s blood for a few days while he waited for a human liver transplant, they say.

The man has since received a human liver and is recovering well, says Lin Wang, one of the surgeons who led the procedure in January at Xijing Hospital of the Air Force Medical University in Xi’an, China. Wang says his team plans to submit the results to a peer-reviewed journal.

Proponents of transplanting genetically modified animal organs into people, a procedure called xenotransplantation, hope that the method could reduce the number of people who die while waiting for a human organ. At least a dozen people in the United States and China have received pig organs, including hearts, kidneys, livers and a thymus – and clinical trials are under way in both nations. But, organ transplants are high-risk surgeries and recipients must take immunosuppressants for the rest of their lives. In the latest surgery, the recipient was connected to a pig liver outside their body — a procedure called extracorporeal perfusion.

The procedure is a bridging therapy that allows a person’s organs to recover, and it can be lifesaving for people who are too sick to wait for a human donor organ without intervention, says Wayne Hawthorne, a surgeon and transplant researcher at the University of Sydney in Australia.

‘Remarkable’

Extracorporeal perfusion using pig organs has been performed since the 1990s, but the development of genetically modified pig organs that are more compatible with people reduces the risk of organ rejection. A US team have connected at least four clinically dead people to external, genetically modified pig livers1. That surgeons in China have been able to do this in a living person is “a remarkable achievement,” adds Hawthorne.

Muhammad Mohiuddin, a clinician-researcher at the University of Maryland School of Medicine in Baltimore, who led the first pig-heart transplant into a living person in 2022, agrees that the technology could be lifesaving.

Hawthorne and Mohiuddin say they are eager to know more details about the surgery in China, including the amount of immune-suppressing therapy that was used, how the man’s health changed over time and how long could someone be connected to the external liver. Results of liver-function tests before, during and after the surgery will also be needed if the team publishes its findings, Hawthorne adds. “This is all the basics for a liver transplant paper.”

The recipient in China had chronic hepatitis B infection, a serious liver illness, and damage caused by alcohol, which led to a sudden deterioration in his liver function, says Wang. He had been hospitalized in Shanghai, China, for a month before Wang’s team treated him. Without a donor organ available, the surgeons decided — with consent from the man and his family — to test whether a pig liver could take over the functions of his failing liver.

The pig liver contained six genetic modifications, says Wang, and was supplied by the company ClonOrgan Biotechnology in Chengdu, China. The six genetic modifications included three deactivated pig genes and introduced three genes that produce human proteins, to reduce the risk of the recipient rejecting the organ.

External filter

The surgeons stitched tubes to a vein in the man’s leg, connecting him to a perfusion device containing the pig liver. His blood was redirected through the pig liver to remove harmful waste products that build up owing to liver failure. The physicians said that there were no signs that the organ was being rejected, and that the man’s own liver function began to improve.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant medical breakthrough where a genetically modified pig liver was successfully used to sustain a living person with liver failure until a human transplant was available. This advancement in xenotransplantation has broad implications for reducing organ transplant wait times and saving lives globally. The story provides detailed context about the procedure, its significance, and the potential impact on the field of organ transplantation, fulfilling the criteria for inspiring good news.
articles.personality_title: Genetically modified pig liver supports man until human transplant
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Many people with liver failure wait a long time for a human organ transplant. Sometimes, they get very sick and need help while waiting. Doctors have been trying to use animal organs, especially from pigs, to support patients temporarily. This is called xenotransplantation.

**What happened** – In January 2026, doctors in Xi’an, China, connected a 56-year-old man with liver failure to a genetically modified pig liver outside his body. This pig liver cleaned his blood for several days until a human liver was available for transplant. The pig liver had six genetic changes to make it safer and less likely to be rejected by the man’s body. The man is now recovering well after receiving the human liver.

**Impact** – This is the first time a living person has been supported by a genetically modified pig liver in this way, showing that it can work as a bridge to human transplants. It could help many people survive while waiting for a donor organ. Using pig organs that are genetically changed reduces the chances of rejection and could ease the shortage of human organs.

**What’s next step** – The medical team plans to share detailed results in a scientific journal. Other researchers want to learn more about how long this method can work and how much medicine is needed to stop rejection. This could lead to more trials and possibly wider use of pig organs to save lives.

**One-sentence takeaway** – For the first time, a genetically modified pig liver successfully kept a man alive until he received a human liver transplant, marking a new step in organ replacement treatment.
articles.personality_title_fr: Un foie de porc génétiquement modifié maintient un homme en vie en attendant une greffe humaine
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – De nombreuses personnes atteintes d’insuffisance hépatique attendent longtemps une greffe de foie humain. Parfois, leur état devient très grave et elles ont besoin d’aide en attendant. Les médecins essaient d’utiliser des organes d’animaux, notamment de porc, pour soutenir temporairement ces patients. Cela s’appelle la xénogreffe.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – En janvier 2026, des médecins à Xi’an, en Chine, ont connecté un homme de 56 ans souffrant d’insuffisance hépatique à un foie de porc génétiquement modifié, placé à l’extérieur de son corps. Ce foie de porc a filtré son sang pendant plusieurs jours jusqu’à ce qu’un foie humain soit disponible pour la greffe. Le foie de porc portait six modifications génétiques pour le rendre plus sûr et moins rejeté par le corps de l’homme. L’homme se remet bien après avoir reçu le foie humain.

**Impact** – C’est la première fois qu’une personne vivante est soutenue de cette manière par un foie de porc génétiquement modifié, montrant que cela peut fonctionner comme solution temporaire avant une greffe humaine. Cela pourrait aider de nombreuses personnes à survivre en attendant un organe donneur. L’utilisation d’organes porcins modifiés réduit les risques de rejet et pourrait diminuer la pénurie d’organes humains.

**Prochaine étape** – L’équipe médicale prévoit de publier les résultats détaillés dans une revue scientifique. D’autres chercheurs souhaitent en savoir plus sur la durée possible de cette méthode et sur la quantité de médicaments nécessaires pour éviter le rejet. Cela pourrait conduire à plus d’essais et à une utilisation plus large des organes de porc pour sauver des vies.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – Pour la première fois, un foie de porc génétiquement modifié a maintenu un homme en vie jusqu’à ce qu’il reçoive une greffe de foie humain, marquant une avancée dans le traitement de remplacement d’organes.
articles.personality_title_es: Hígado de cerdo modificado genéticamente mantiene vivo a un hombre hasta el trasplante humano
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Muchas personas con insuficiencia hepática esperan mucho tiempo para un trasplante de hígado humano. A veces, su salud empeora y necesitan ayuda mientras esperan. Los médicos han intentado usar órganos de animales, especialmente de cerdos, para apoyar temporalmente a los pacientes. Esto se llama xenotrasplante.

**Qué pasó** – En enero de 2026, médicos en Xi’an, China, conectaron a un hombre de 56 años con insuficiencia hepática a un hígado de cerdo modificado genéticamente fuera de su cuerpo. Este hígado de cerdo filtró su sangre durante varios días hasta que estuvo disponible un hígado humano para trasplante. El hígado tenía seis modificaciones genéticas para hacerlo más seguro y menos probable que el cuerpo lo rechazara. El hombre se está recuperando bien tras recibir el hígado humano.

**Impacto** – Es la primera vez que una persona viva es sostenida de esta manera con un hígado de cerdo modificado genéticamente, demostrando que puede funcionar como un puente hasta el trasplante humano. Esto podría ayudar a muchas personas a sobrevivir mientras esperan un órgano donante. Usar órganos de cerdo modificados reduce el riesgo de rechazo y podría aliviar la escasez de órganos humanos.

**Próximo paso** – El equipo médico planea publicar resultados detallados en una revista científica. Otros investigadores quieren saber cuánto tiempo puede durar este método y cuánta medicina se necesita para evitar el rechazo. Esto podría llevar a más ensayos y a un uso más amplio de órganos de cerdo para salvar vidas.

**Resumen en una frase** – Por primera vez, un hígado de cerdo modificado genéticamente mantuvo vivo a un hombre hasta que recibió un trasplante de hígado humano, marcando un nuevo avance en el tratamiento de reemplazo de órganos.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a sturdy, anatomically stylized pig liver gently cradled within a pair of glowing, abstract human hands connected by delicate, translucent tubes to a silhouette of a resting man, set against a soft, neutral background symbolizing healing and life support.

articles.title: Peanut-processing microbes ward off dangerous allergic shock
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 06 March 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00668-9Scientists identify bacteria that digest allergy-triggering compounds in peanuts, which can be life-threatening to those with allergies.
articles.content: Thank you for visiting nature.com. You are using a browser version with limited support for CSS. To obtain the best experience, we recommend you use a more up to date browser (or turn off compatibility mode in Internet Explorer). In the meantime, to ensure continued support, we are displaying the site without styles and JavaScript.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a scientific breakthrough identifying bacteria that can digest allergy-triggering compounds in peanuts, potentially preventing life-threatening allergic reactions. This has significant positive real-world impact for many people with peanut allergies, offering a promising new approach to allergy management and safety.
articles.personality_title: New bacteria found that can break down peanut allergens
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Peanut allergies affect millions of people worldwide and can cause dangerous reactions called allergic shocks. These reactions happen because certain compounds in peanuts trigger the body's immune system.

**What happened** – Scientists discovered special bacteria that can digest the allergy-causing compounds in peanuts. This discovery was published in the journal Nature on March 6, 2026. The bacteria work by breaking down the harmful parts of peanuts before they can cause allergic reactions.

**Impact** – This finding is important because it may help create new ways to make peanuts safer for people with allergies. Instead of avoiding peanuts completely, these bacteria could be used to treat peanuts or help people’s bodies handle peanut allergens better, reducing the risk of severe allergic shocks.

**What's next step** – Researchers will now work on how to use these bacteria safely and effectively. They might develop treatments or food processing methods that include the bacteria to reduce peanut allergens in foods.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists have found bacteria that can break down peanut allergens, offering a new way to protect people with peanut allergies from dangerous reactions.
articles.personality_title_fr: De nouvelles bactéries capables de décomposer les allergènes des arachides
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les allergies aux arachides touchent des millions de personnes dans le monde et peuvent provoquer des réactions graves appelées chocs allergiques. Ces réactions se produisent parce que certaines substances dans les arachides déclenchent le système immunitaire.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des scientifiques ont découvert des bactéries spéciales capables de digérer les composés responsables des allergies dans les arachides. Cette découverte a été publiée dans la revue Nature le 6 mars 2026. Ces bactéries agissent en détruisant les parties nocives des arachides avant qu'elles ne provoquent une réaction allergique.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est importante car elle pourrait permettre de rendre les arachides plus sûres pour les personnes allergiques. Au lieu d'éviter complètement les arachides, ces bactéries pourraient être utilisées pour traiter les arachides ou aider le corps à mieux gérer les allergènes, réduisant ainsi le risque de chocs allergiques graves.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs vont maintenant travailler sur la manière d'utiliser ces bactéries de façon sûre et efficace. Ils pourraient développer des traitements ou des méthodes de transformation des aliments qui incluent ces bactéries pour diminuer les allergènes des arachides.

**En une phrase** – Des scientifiques ont découvert des bactéries capables de décomposer les allergènes des arachides, offrant une nouvelle solution pour protéger les personnes allergiques contre les réactions dangereuses.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevas bacterias descubiertas que descomponen alérgenos del maní
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Las alergias al maní afectan a millones de personas en todo el mundo y pueden causar reacciones graves llamadas shock alérgico. Estas reacciones ocurren porque ciertos compuestos en el maní activan el sistema inmunológico.

**Qué pasó** – Científicos encontraron bacterias especiales que pueden digerir los compuestos que causan alergias en el maní. Este descubrimiento se publicó en la revista Nature el 6 de marzo de 2026. Las bacterias funcionan descomponiendo las partes dañinas del maní antes de que puedan causar reacciones alérgicas.

**Impacto** – Este hallazgo es importante porque podría ayudar a crear nuevas formas de hacer que el maní sea más seguro para las personas alérgicas. En lugar de evitar el maní por completo, estas bacterias podrían usarse para tratar el maní o ayudar al cuerpo a manejar mejor los alérgenos, reduciendo el riesgo de shocks alérgicos graves.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores ahora trabajarán en cómo usar estas bacterias de manera segura y efectiva. Podrían desarrollar tratamientos o métodos de procesamiento de alimentos que incluyan las bacterias para reducir los alérgenos del maní.

**Una frase clave** – Los científicos han encontrado bacterias que pueden descomponer alérgenos del maní, ofreciendo una nueva forma de proteger a las personas alérgicas de reacciones peligrosas.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of friendly, glowing microbes gently surrounding and protecting a cluster of peanuts, with soft natural earth tones and a subtle light aura symbolizing safety and healing.
